topic: Australia road scaffold validation — post-ISS-1467/1468 patch review (Giacomin venue corrected; sub-1% comparison re-statted to mean-ratio vs same-hemisphere 10.2%)
change_summary: Two c4 holes closed: (H1) 'Giacomin & Levinson 2015, Transportation Planning and Technology' corrected to 'Environment and Planning B: Planning and Design' — venue now matches the paper's real citation (verified via SAGE Journals / ideas.repec.org / experts.umn.edu). (H2) 'sub-1% mean-ratio prediction errors ... versus V13 >=4 free parameters for 6.2%' rephrased to 'sub-1% mean-ratio prediction errors against observed road distances with a single free parameter (V13's same-hemisphere mean error on this same metric is 10.2%, with >=4 free parameters)' — the comparator is now same-statistic (mean-ratio vs mean-ratio). detail_evidence length grew 5407 -> 5708 (+301). Other fields unchanged; verdict Misleading held.
our_argument_summary: Verdict: Misleading. Core critique unchanged from c3/c4: (1) road excess over geodesic is terrain-driven, not topology (90-Mile Straight <0.4% deviation anchor); (2) 6.2% RMSE is ~5 orders of magnitude worse than sub-meter geodesy (250,000x); (3) AGD66->GDA94 is a routine regional->geocentric datum improvement; (4) V13 parameters (a = pi x R_Earth = 20,015 km) are calibrated against globe distances, making validation circular; (5) selected 12 roads + 9 cross-links are an undisclosed sample from a much larger route universe. c5 delta: ISS-1467 (Giacomin venue) and ISS-1468 (mean-ratio vs RMSE statistic mismatch) are now closed in detail_evidence. The two key c4 moderate holes are resolved. Remaining picks: (i) the co-cited Huber & Rust 2016 venue, which was 'unchecked' in c4, turns out to be wrong (Stata Journal, not R Journal) — same class of error we just closed on Giacomin; (ii) the summary-table finding contains a factual geography error ('same-hemisphere routes (e.g. Australia to UK)' — Australia-UK is cross-equatorial in both globe and V13 two-zone frames) and a statistic conflation (finding asserts same-hemisphere routes 'also diverge at 6.2%', but the detail says same-hemisphere mean error is 10.2%, not 6.2%); (iii) the two-citation counter-model still doesn't directly support the specific 'sub-1% mean-ratio with one free parameter' claim — Giacomin/Levinson 2015 documents metropolitan circuity temporal trends, Huber/Rust 2016 is a Stata tooling paper, neither is a published fit-result source for the single-parameter sub-1% number; (iv) carryover c3/c4 holes #6 ('thousands of possible road pairs') and 0.4%-vs-<0.01% anchor looseness remain live but low-priority.

relevant_files: raw-text/04-coordinates.txt (V12 snapshot; V13 appeared after this raw-text capture)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Unchanged since c3. V13 two-zone topology: disc radius a = 20,015 km ~ pi x R_Earth; equatorial ring at 14,105 km; refractive profile n(r) with H(r) = 8,537 x exp(-r/8,619) km. 6.2% cross-equatorial RMSE and 10.2% same-hemisphere mean error are static published dome-site claims, not live-monitored.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No monitoring automation for WIN-069. The ISS-1467/1468 patches were prose substring replacements in detail_evidence; they do not introduce any new monitoring or change any numerical claim in our review text.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Unchanged. V13 has >=4 free parameters (H_0, lambda, equatorial ring radius, angular scaling factor ~0.9941) that can be retuned to reduce 6.2% without new dome physics. The same-hemisphere mean-ratio reframing in the new text uses 10.2% as the V13 number; this is the figure the dome publishes, not a computation we verified independently.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None introduced by the c5 patches. The restatted comparator ('same-hemisphere mean error on this same metric is 10.2%') matches the detail_evidence's own earlier-paragraph statement of 10.2% same-hemisphere mean error; internally consistent now.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Unchanged since c3. No monitor.py entry fetches or compares road-distance data for WIN-069; it is a static published claim derived from a selected 12-road / 9-cross-link sample on the dome site.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Unchanged. Standard physics has no 'road-distance-vs-geodesic' predictive framework; road excess is treated as a topography / road-network engineering question (the circuity literature cited now). V13 invents a topology-based explanation rather than relabeling a standard one.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Unchanged. Australian road distances, WGS84 inter-city distances, Nullarbor segment lengths, and AGD66->GDA94 datum history all predate V13 by decades; the dome adopted known quantities and labeled matching them as confirmation.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: Unchanged from c4. V13 topology + refractive profile are dome-derived; numerical parameters are fit to globe data (AGD66->GDA94), but the geometric framework (ring/toroidal, V13) originates from dome model structure. Consistent with EXP-054 verdict boilerplate and WIN-057.

holes_found.description: NEW CITATION-INTEGRITY ERROR (same class as ISS-1467 we just fixed): Huber & Rust 2016 is cited as 'R Journal' but the paper was actually published in The Stata Journal, vol. 16(2), pp. 416-423 (2016), DOI 10.1177/1536867X1601600209. Verified by web search: SAGE Journals listing (journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/1536867X1601600209), EconPapers (RePEc:tsj:stataj:y:16:y:2016:i:2:p:416-423), Semantic Scholar, ResearchGate — all list the same publication in The Stata Journal. The paper is 'Calculate travel time and distance with OpenStreetMap data using the Open Source Routing Machine (OSRM)' — an `osrmtime` Stata command paper, not an R Journal vignette. This was already listed as 'unchecked' in the c4 citation_check. The EXP-231 patch that introduced this citation closed hole #4 ('unsourced counter-model') but simultaneously created a NEW citation-integrity hole of exactly the same kind we just spent a cycle closing on Giacomin & Levinson. A dome defender who runs any automated citation-verification pass will flag this trivially: author+year+title resolves to Stata Journal with DOI 10.1177/1536867X1601600209, not to R Journal. Our meta-argument ('our citations verify, theirs do not' — Part 3 and Part 10) is weaker in c5 than it was before the c4 fix set, because we replaced one verifiable-bad citation with one right + one wrong.
holes_found.recommendation: One-word venue fix in detail_evidence: change 'Huber & Rust 2016, R Journal' to 'Huber & Rust 2016, The Stata Journal'. While you are there, also check whether Huber & Rust 2016 actually supports the numerical claim being attached to it (see next hole) — the paper is a Stata-tooling paper about the `osrmtime` command; the 'detour-index 1.05-1.20 / sub-1% mean-ratio / single-parameter fit' framing is not the paper's contribution. If the claim cannot be attributed to Huber & Rust 2016, swap the tooling-paper citation for an actual empirical circuity result (Levinson & El-Geneidy 2009, Barthelemy 2011 Phys Reports network-geometry review, or Boeing 2019 for morphology/circuity of street networks). Sagarra et al. 2015 and Giacomin & Levinson 2015 collectively do carry the ~1.05-1.25 detour-index empirical range; citing Huber & Rust as a co-anchor for that range overstates what that specific paper documents.

holes_found.description: FINDING CONTAINS A FACTUAL GEOGRAPHY ERROR AND A STATISTIC CONFLATION. The summary-table finding reads: '6.2% RMSE is orders of magnitude worse than sub-meter geodesy; same-hemisphere routes (e.g. Australia to UK) also diverge at 6.2%, which V13 predicts should be its best case. AGD66->GDA94 shift is routine datum improvement, not evidence of topology.' Two problems: (a) Australia (~10 deg S to ~45 deg S) and the UK (~50 deg N to ~60 deg N) are on opposite sides of the equator — any Australia-UK route is CROSS-EQUATORIAL, not same-hemisphere. This is true under both the ordinary geographic definition AND under V13's two-zone topology (Australia is in the outer ring-zone, UK is in the inner disc-zone, per V13's disc-radius = pi x R_Earth mapping). So the parenthetical example is factually incorrect regardless of which framing a reader applies. (b) The detail_evidence text says '6.2% cross-equatorial RMSE and 10.2% same-hemisphere mean error' — these are two different statistics on two different route classes. The finding collapses them into a single 6.2% figure attributed to same-hemisphere routes, which contradicts the detail paragraph directly below. Summary-table text is what readers see first and many never expand; a finding that both names the wrong geography AND conflicts with its own detail paragraph is directly citable as 'your one-line summary contradicts your own paragraph, and your example is wrong.' This survived c3/c4 unflagged because the c3/c4 finding rewrites were scoped to the '10,000x' framing (ISS-1434), not the hemisphere-qualifier framing.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite the finding so it matches the detail_evidence. Suggested text: '6.2% cross-equatorial RMSE is ~5 orders of magnitude worse than sub-meter geodesy; same-hemisphere routes (V13 best case) still show 10.2% mean error. AGD66->GDA94 shift is routine regional-to-geocentric datum improvement, not evidence of topology.' This (i) uses 'cross-equatorial' to label the 6.2% RMSE consistent with the detail, (ii) drops the 'Australia to UK' example — if a concrete example is needed, 'Perth-Sydney' or 'Melbourne-Perth' would be genuinely same-hemisphere same-continent, and the dome's published 10 routes include intra-Australian legs, (iii) uses 10.2% for same-hemisphere consistent with the detail. If the 'V13 predicts should be its best case' framing is rhetorically important, keep it — the point is that V13 still loses on its own strongest route class.

holes_found.description: CITATIONS DO NOT DIRECTLY SUPPORT THE SPECIFIC NUMERICAL CLAIM. The patched sentence reads '...the detour-index / road-circuity literature (Huber & Rust 2016, R Journal; Giacomin & Levinson 2015, Environment and Planning B: Planning and Design) documents typical road-network detour indices of ~1.05-1.20 as a terrain-driven function, producing sub-1% mean-ratio prediction errors against observed road distances with a single free parameter.' Actual paper contents (verified via abstracts): Giacomin & Levinson 2015, 'Road network circuity in metropolitan areas,' Environment and Planning B 42(6):1040-1053 — measures circuity trends across 51 US MSAs from 1990-2010 and reports that overall circuity has INCREASED over time; it does not present a predictive 'single-parameter fit with sub-1% prediction error.' Huber & Rust 2016 (correctly, Stata Journal 16(2):416-423) — introduces the `osrmtime` Stata command for computing travel time / distance; it does not report a detour-index prediction fit at all. So the two cited papers jointly establish that circuity is a studied empirical quantity in the 1.05-1.25 range, but they do NOT establish 'sub-1% mean-ratio prediction error with a single free parameter' — that claim needs its own citation or a derivation from the cited data. This is a moderate hole because the argument is probably true (a one-parameter mean-ratio fit calibrated on MSA circuity data does achieve sub-1% mean-ratio error by construction, but we should cite where that was demonstrated, not assume it).
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) soften the claim to 'typical detour indices of ~1.05-1.20' and drop the 'sub-1% mean-ratio prediction error / single free parameter' quantifier (the remaining comparison 'same-hemisphere V13 mean error is 10.2%' is itself damning without needing the sub-1% baseline); or (b) keep the quantifier but cite a paper that actually reports it — Ballou et al. 2002 (circuity factor convention in logistics), Demetriades & Vergados 2017 (circuity factor empirical fits), or Levinson & El-Geneidy 2009 ('The minimum circuity frontier and the journey to work,' Regional Science and Urban Economics 39(6):732-738) which explicitly fits circuity functions. Without that, the 'sub-1% single-parameter' framing is our assertion, not a cited result, and a careful defender will say 'you did to us exactly what you accused V13 of: you stated a number without a source and expected it to pass.'

holes_found.description: POSSIBLE CIRCULARITY IN 'SINGLE FREE PARAMETER' COMPARISON — SAME FAILURE MODE WE ACCUSE V13 OF. If a circuity model's 'single free parameter' is the mean detour index fitted on the same road-network data it then 'predicts,' the resulting sub-1% mean-ratio error is tautological (fitting the mean to the data gives zero mean-ratio residual by construction; sub-1% is the noise around that). This is structurally identical to the V13 circularity critique we make in paragraph 5 of detail_evidence: 'dome parameters are set to approximately reproduce globe distances, then the approximate reproduction is claimed as confirmation.' Unless the circuity model we cite is out-of-sample (e.g., fit on US MSA data, then tested on Australian transcontinental routes — which Giacomin & Levinson 2015 does not do), the comparison is not a fair head-to-head. A smart dome defender will say: 'You demand out-of-sample validation from us and give us a calibrated in-sample fit as your counter-example.' The c4 review flagged the statistic mismatch; the c5 patch fixed the statistic but did not address whether the new same-statistic comparison avoids the calibration circularity.
holes_found.recommendation: Two options, both small. (a) Add a parenthetical distinguishing in-sample calibration from out-of-sample prediction: 'The circuity literature fits detour-index distributions on large road-network samples; the sub-1% residual is an in-sample fit, but V13's 10.2% same-hemisphere mean error remains worse even under that in-sample-friendly framing.' (b) Drop the 'sub-1% mean-ratio' quantifier and lead with the qualitative point: standard road-network engineering has a well-tested one-parameter (mean detour index) framework that accounts for ~90%+ of road excess; V13 adds four parameters and still loses. Either variant closes the defender's circularity counterpunch.

holes_found.description: CARRYOVER from c3 hole #6 / c4 carryover — 'thousands of possible Australian road pairs' language still live. detail_evidence's final paragraph reads '...the dome cites 12 roads and 9 cross-links as its validation sample from thousands of possible Australian road pairs. No selection criteria are disclosed.' The 'thousands' framing is argumentatively correct but gives a defender a number to haggle with ('Most of those thousands are short suburban roads and irrelevant to continental-scale topology testing'). This was deferred in c4 for EXP-231 scoping; still deferred in c5's patch scope. No urgency; low-priority micro-patch as noted in c4.
holes_found.recommendation: Unchanged from c4: replace 'thousands of possible Australian road pairs' with 'the larger set of major transcontinental and inter-city Australian road pairs' or a specific defensible count (e.g., '~50 inter-city pairs among Australia's 15 largest cities'). Roll into the next WIN-069 minor cleanup EXP.

holes_found.description: CARRYOVER from c4 minor hole — 0.4% anchor still a loose upper bound. 90-Mile Straight's actual geodesic deviation on WGS84 is well under 0.01% (sub-meter ellipsoid-chord separation); '<0.4%' matches the TLDR figure but is ~40x looser than the true geometric bound. Low priority; the argument still goes the right way at 0.4%. Noted in c4; no change in c5.
holes_found.recommendation: Unchanged from c4: if any TLDR/detail pass touches WIN-069 again, tighten '<0.4%' to '<0.01%' in the detail_evidence sentence; update the TLDRs correspondingly. Otherwise leave as-is.

stronger_arguments.description: The Huber & Rust venue error IS a better argument against ourselves than we initially realized — it is now the c5 instance of exactly the citation pattern we catch in the dome (WIN-008, WIN-009, WIN-014, and previously WIN-069 itself at c4 with the Giacomin venue). The rhetorical value of fixing it is meta: a single-pass citation clean across the WIN-069 counter-model paragraph preserves the 'our citations verify, theirs do not' framing. Cost to fix: one word (R Journal -> The Stata Journal).
sources: Curmudgeon c1/c2/c4 track record on citation-integrity holes in WIN-008/009/014/069

stronger_arguments.description: The same-hemisphere/cross-equatorial finding-vs-detail contradiction argument can be turned into a positive for our review. Currently our finding says '6.2% RMSE' and our detail says '6.2% cross-equatorial RMSE / 10.2% same-hemisphere mean error' — different numbers on different route classes. Rewriting the finding to 'same-hemisphere routes still show 10.2% mean error' highlights that V13 LOSES EVEN ON ITS BEST ROUTE CLASS. That is a stronger finding than the current text, which conflates the two metrics and thereby makes the summary look arithmetically sloppy. Swap: change is purely editorial; no new data or sources required.
sources: WIN-069 detail_evidence paragraph 2 (6.2% cross-eq RMSE, 10.2% same-hem mean error) — internal consistency

stronger_arguments.description: If the citation-integrity cleanup route (hole 1) is taken, it is trivial to piggyback hole 3's citation strengthening by adding Levinson & El-Geneidy 2009, Regional Science and Urban Economics 39(6):732-738 ('The minimum circuity frontier and the journey to work'). That paper explicitly fits circuity functions across US MSAs and can anchor the 'sub-1% mean-ratio with a single parameter' claim directly — plus D. Levinson appears on both our citations then, which is a verifiable-author continuity signal to readers doing source checks.
sources: Levinson & El-Geneidy 2009 (Regional Science and Urban Economics)

kernel_of_truth.description: The ISS-1467 and ISS-1468 patches did what they said. Giacomin & Levinson's venue is correctly stated now, and the statistic comparison is same-statistic (mean-ratio vs mean-ratio). The re-pivot to 10.2% same-hemisphere mean error inside the comparative clause is strategically right: it attacks V13 on its strongest route class, where V13 is supposed to be at its best. On defense_survives, the c4 projected endpoint was 1 — we are now at 3 because two new issues (Huber venue; finding-vs-detail contradiction) showed up that were unforeseen at c4.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The verdict 'Misleading' is robust through all of these patches — the core arithmetic (6.2% residual on 4,000 km = 250 km of error; sub-meter geodesy is 250,000x better) is unchanged and is doing the argumentative work. The patches are editorial integrity matters, not verdict-disturbing. But the curmudgeon standard is 'bulletproof,' and a text that contradicts its own paragraph in its first line is not bulletproof — so the patches still need another pass.

deeper_analysis: c5 closes two of the four open c4 holes (Giacomin venue; RMSE-vs-mean-ratio statistic mismatch) and leaves the remaining two as long-deferred minor items. On net the text is better than it was at c4. But the c5 patch set also surfaced two new issues that c4 had not named: (a) the co-cited Huber & Rust 2016 venue is wrong — 'R Journal' should be 'The Stata Journal' — so we replaced one verifiable-bad citation with one right + one wrong; (b) the summary-table finding is both geographically wrong ('Australia to UK' = cross-equatorial, not same-hemisphere) and statistically inconsistent with its own detail paragraph ('6.2%' for same-hemisphere when the detail says 10.2%). Both are one-edit fixes. A third c5 concern is that the citations as attached do not directly support the specific 'sub-1% mean-ratio with a single free parameter' quantitative claim — Giacomin & Levinson 2015 is a temporal circuity trend study, Huber & Rust 2016 is a Stata tool paper. Either the quantifier should be softened to 'circuity models routinely outperform V13 on the mean-ratio statistic' or a new citation (Levinson & El-Geneidy 2009 is a direct fit) should be added. And a fourth c5 observation is that, unless we are careful about in-sample vs out-of-sample framing, the 'single free parameter' comparison replicates the V13 circularity we criticize — fitting the mean detour index on the same sample you then 'predict' gives sub-1% mean-ratio residual by construction. One parenthetical closes that escape. Verdict trajectory: 'Misleading' holds with confidence 0.88 (vs 0.90 at c4; slight drop because the new citation-integrity error is structurally the same class we catch in the dome, which costs meta-credibility until patched). After the two one-edit patches land, c5 defense_survives drops from 3 back to 1.

citations_verified: Giacomin & Levinson 2015, 'Road network circuity in metropolitan areas,' Environment and Planning B: Planning and Design 42(6):1040-1053 — venue NOW CORRECTLY CITED (SAGE Journals DOI 10.1068/b130131p; experts.umn.edu/en/publications/road-network-circuity-in-metropolitan-areas; ideas.repec.org/a/sae/envirb/v42y2015i6p1040-1053.html all confirm).
90-Mile Straight 146.6 km, Balladonia to Caiguna — segment length and endpoints match published records; road-to-geodesic deviation <0.4% remains a correct (loose) upper bound.
a = 20,015 km = pi x R_Earth arithmetic — pi x 6,371 = 20,015.6 km (WGS84 mean), matches stated value to rounding.
5 orders of magnitude vs sub-meter: 6.2% of 4,000 km = 248 km / 1 m = 2.48 x 10^5 ~ 250,000x, consistent with '~5 orders of magnitude' in detail_evidence.
citations_failed: Huber & Rust 2016, 'R Journal' — WRONG VENUE. The paper 'Calculate travel time and distance with OpenStreetMap data using the Open Source Routing Machine (OSRM)' by Stephan Huber and Christoph Rust was published in The Stata Journal, vol. 16, no. 2, pp. 416-423 (2016), DOI 10.1177/1536867X1601600209. Verified via SAGE Journals, EconPapers (RePEc:tsj:stataj:y:16:y:2016:i:2:p:416-423), Semantic Scholar, ResearchGate, and University of Regensburg epub server. Same class of error as the ISS-1467 Giacomin error that this c5 patch cycle closed. This citation was listed as 'unchecked' in the c4 review; it is now checked and wrong.
citations_unchecked: Internal anchor #posthoc-standard (Part 3.1b) — depends on built HTML.
Kill-Shot #1 internal cross-reference in detail_verdict_text — depends on section-rendering state.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: No new cross-WIN inconsistency introduced by the c5 patches. The EW-scale / H(r) geometry continues to be consistent with WIN-057's treatment of V13 topology; WIN-027's quadratic distance-excess relabel remains a distinct tag combination (relabels_standard=true / derives_from_dome=false) that does not reference V13 topology directly. One latent cross-WIN concern: if we ever fix the 'same-hemisphere (e.g. Australia to UK)' finding, we should verify that WIN-027's similarly-worded 'southern' framing does not have the same mis-example — a quick look in wins.json WIN-027 shows it uses latitude-bucketed descriptions and does not include a cross-equatorial example mislabeled as same-hemisphere, so WIN-027 is clean.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A c5 dome defender steelman: 'You fixed the Giacomin venue — good. But your c5 text still cites Huber & Rust 2016 as R Journal, and that paper is actually in The Stata Journal (DOI 10.1177/1536867X1601600209). Two-for-one in the same paragraph. Meanwhile your summary-table finding says same-hemisphere routes diverge at 6.2% using Australia-to-UK as the example. Australia-UK is cross-equatorial under any reasonable framing (globe or V13 two-zone), and your own detail paragraph says same-hemisphere mean error is 10.2%, not 6.2%. Your summary contradicts your own paragraph and your example is geographically wrong. Finally, your `sub-1% mean-ratio prediction error with a single free parameter` framing is not supported by either cited paper — Giacomin & Levinson is a temporal trend study of metropolitan circuity, Huber & Rust is a Stata tooling paper. You demand out-of-sample pre-registration from us; you cite a non-supporting paper for your own one-parameter counter-model. The 6.2% vs sub-meter geodesy headline arithmetic stands; the rigor-of-our-review framing you lean on does not.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Three small edits close all three sub-arguments. (1) One-word venue fix: 'R Journal' -> 'The Stata Journal' in detail_evidence. (2) Finding rewrite to 'same-hemisphere routes (V13 best case) still show 10.2% mean error' — drop the Australia-UK example or replace with Perth-Sydney/Melbourne-Perth. (3) Either soften the 'sub-1% mean-ratio with a single free parameter' quantifier or add Levinson & El-Geneidy 2009 as a direct supporting citation. After all three, defense_survives drops to 1 (the residual is rhetorical, not citable).

claims_checked: Giacomin & Levinson 2015 venue = Environment and Planning B: Planning and Design, 42(6):1040-1053 — correct per ISS-1467 fix (verified).
Huber & Rust 2016 venue = R Journal — INCORRECT. Actual venue = The Stata Journal, 16(2):416-423 (verified).
6.2% cross-equatorial RMSE / 10.2% same-hemisphere mean error — these are two different statistics on two different route classes per the detail paragraph; the finding conflates them.
Australia (~10 deg S to ~45 deg S) and UK (~50 deg N to ~60 deg N) — unambiguously on opposite sides of equator, so 'Australia to UK' is cross-equatorial, not same-hemisphere.
a = pi x R_Earth = 20,015 km — matches pi x 6,371 = 20,015.6 km.
250,000x vs sub-meter: 6.2% x 4,000 km = 248 km; 248 km / 1 m = 2.48 x 10^5 — correct to rounding, '~5 orders of magnitude' is a conservative framing.
10.2% on same-hemisphere routes vs sub-1% circuity-model fit — same-statistic now, but circuity fit is in-sample and may be circular (see hole 4).

90-Mile Straight geometry: 146.6 km physical straight road at ~S 32 deg, ~1.9 deg longitude span; ellipsoid geodesic-vs-chord deviation ~1 m over 150 km => ratio <1e-5 (<0.001%). The '<0.4%' figure is a correct upper bound but ~40x loose vs the true geometric bound.
quantitative_verification.computations: Huber & Rust venue verification: web search returned 10 independent sources (SAGE Journals, SSRN, ResearchGate, Semantic Scholar, EconPapers, University of Regensburg pred, University of Regensburg epub, IDEAS, Scite, rutasoptimas.github.io) all confirming The Stata Journal 16(2):416-423, DOI 10.1177/1536867X1601600209, for the paper 'Calculate travel time and distance with OpenStreetMap data using the Open Source Routing Machine (OSRM)' by Huber & Rust 2016. No R Journal entry exists for this author/year/title combination. The paper's content is a Stata command (`osrmtime`) for calculating travel time/distance, not an empirical circuity fit. Australia/UK hemisphere check: Australia spans roughly 10 deg S (Cape York) to 44 deg S (Tasmania); UK spans roughly 50 deg N (Cornwall) to 60 deg N (Shetland). No overlap, both ranges on opposite sides of the equator. Any Australia-UK great-circle or road route crosses 0 deg latitude. Under V13's two-zone topology (inner disc = Northern Hemisphere mapping, outer ring = Southern Hemisphere mapping via the dome's a = pi x R_Earth equation), Australia maps to the outer ring and UK to the inner disc — again cross-zone. So 'Australia to UK' cannot serve as a same-hemisphere example in either framework.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: (1) Huber & Rust 2016 venue ('R Journal') is factually wrong; correct venue is The Stata Journal, vol. 16(2), pp. 416-423 (2016). (2) Finding's 'same-hemisphere routes (e.g. Australia to UK) also diverge at 6.2%' conflicts with the detail's 10.2% same-hemisphere mean error and picks a cross-equatorial example to illustrate same-hemisphere — both are editorial errors surviving the c4/c5 patch cycles because scoped edits did not touch the finding's hemisphere qualifier. (3) 'Sub-1% mean-ratio prediction error with a single free parameter' is not directly supported by either cited paper as attached; it reads as an uncited quantitative assertion.

notes: queue_id set to 245, matches queue item. queue_pushed_at set to 2026-04-23T11:35:37.470Z, matches queue item. Trigger 'priority-queue-win-rewrite' matches queue item target_type 'win-detail-rewrite'. ISS-1467/1468 fixes verified in current detail_evidence. Two new major holes surfaced this cycle (Huber & Rust venue; finding-vs-detail geography/statistic contradiction) — both are one-edit fixes.
